story:
Ravenmaster Debs is on day 214 of keeping the Tower ravens at home—the superstition intact, the kingdom safe; touch wood. The uniform’s a bit silly; the job isn’t. She runs through the feeding—apples, dog biscuits, raw beef, the odd mouse—and introduces her star: “George,” top bird, clever enough to unzip a bag and pinch what he wants. She tries vocal training for a cheeky “hello.” Instead, the raven fixes her with an eye and says, clear as bell-metal: “A fine handmaiden you are, for the hawks of blood.” Debs laughs it off, makes a theatre joke, but the air shifts.

On the next stream, she admits he’s been “busy.” Fans drop ideas in the chat; the raven watches, silent, until he says, “The moniker of your princes’ lord is not mine. George is not my name.” After a rasping effort—“Eyy-ill”—Debs promises respect and captions it for the stream. Soon it’s settled: Egill. He signs off with, “Farewell, owners of the speech of giants.”

Word spreads. People queue for the “Prophecy Queue”—which Debs thinks should be the “Roasting Row,” because Egill’s beak is sharp. School groups arrive. He greets them: “Children of silk. Feathers too soft for flight.” A visitor coos, “Say hello, birdie!” Egill replies, “Your princes shall taste the apple of Hel before spring.” Nervous laughter. “Do me, do me!” someone else demands. Egill surveys a beard: “Your beard could shelter crows, yet none dare rest there.” When he’s mocked, he counters, “In the fight for the kingdom, your farts shall be louder than your battle horn.” Debs warns: come at your own peril.

Off-shift beneath a bruised sky, even Debs finds herself talking like him. He persists with “handmaiden.” She bristles; he softens: “I set my spear to rest before you.” Online, people say she trained him. She denies it on air. “Ask him yourself,” she says. Egill answers the nation: “The crown sleeps while the speaking stones awaken. The Tower remembers the builders, stone by stone.” Then, to the host: “Your speech buffets the sail like squally winds, and your boat goes nowhere.” The hashtag arrives anyway: #StonesAwaken.

Crowds swell. Debs keeps the line orderly, keeps the ravens safe. Public service announcement: don’t bring bread—it harms birds. She jokes she has enough proper dog biscuits to last “until the coming of Christ.” A visitor in a #StonesAwaken shirt thanks Egill; he answers, “These walls were raised by hands like yours. A conspiracy flies into the day’s fire.” Debs translates for the chat: a “conspiracy” is a group of ravens; the “day’s fire” is the dawn. She asks people to respect opening hours; the comments flood with white stone emojis. She threatens, cheerfully martial, to have “bones to pick.” Egill corrects the title one last time: “The Valkyrie moves through the blood-mist, honour-collector from the storm of shields.” Debs approves. Valkyrie will do.

Through it all, the count holds. The ravens remain. The superstition stands. A working day becomes a saga: a keeper, her flock, the Tower’s stones, and a raven who refuses to be just a mascot—oracle, insulter, historian—speaking in the language of storms and builders while Debs, deadpan and dutiful, keeps the birds fed, the public in line, and the legend alive.
